speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Carol Stulberg, and I have the privilege of interviewing Mr. Nate Shapow in Los Angeles, Wednesday August the 10th, 1994. Mr. Shapow, please give me your name at birth and tell me where and when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Nathan Shapow. I was born in Riga, Latvia, in 1921-- November, 1921. 6. November 6, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names and the names and ages of your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' names was Haya Hodel. That's my mom. And my dad was Chaim Mordechai. The ages of my brothers-- don't remember the ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Efraim and Beryl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was working. He was a hat maker at first. And I don't remember all those, but I think I knew that he was a hat maker. And afterwards he's gotten to be in finance business. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was all along until 1934. In 1934, we went to Israel. Not legally. And we remained with me and my brothers and my mom remained in Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: We packed everything up. We gave our money to-- that time it was-- the name of the organization was Palestinaamt. You know what Palestinaamt is? Palestinaamt was-- like, people went to Israel through to Palestine through Palestinaamt. That's an organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: We packed everything up and we went to go to Romania. Romania was Bucharest and Konstanz. We went to the ship. And we had four ships guards. All three were false, were not right. Ships guards was not right. Just one was the right one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said to me, why don't you go to your father? And I didn't want to go. I refused to go. I was the oldest one. So then we went anyhow through Beirut, and we couldn't get off because my father was not legally. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we had to sell everything we had. My mom sold diamonds, a fur coat. We sold our-- we had a lift with furniture. We sold everything and we went home, back home. And then when the problems starts. Describe to me what happened. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a sick woman and I went to work. I was 14 or 15 years old. Went to work and that was probably by '39, '40, the Russians came in. And they were a year in Riga. I still worked for-- in a factory of salami. Good, what I like. And the Germans came in then. That time, the Germans pushed out the Russians and they came into Riga. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When that occurred, they pushed us in, all of us from 16 to 25, into a small ghetto. And they took children and the older people and the invalids, and they took them to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] places where they kill them. The name was Rumbula. That's the name, but it's still there. And they killed them there. The Latvians, the natives, Gentiles, helped the Germans to kill them. And then we start to work for the Germans. We worked probably till 1944 for the Germans. We had to risk for a piece of bread. We'd been hung for a quarter of a pound butter. They hung-- probably every other day, they hung people from the entrance and exit of the city where we went to work and came from work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was probably almost a year, 1944, when they close the ghetto. Because the ghetto was arming itself with arms to make the last point that we-- and they caught arms in the ghetto. And so they closed the ghetto 1944 then. And they shipped us to Kaiserwelt. Kaiserwelt was a arbeitslager, this, you know, a labor camp. And they send us from there to work in the country. They used to cut-- it's not coal, but it's like coal. It's [INAUDIBLE]. It's like-- it's in the ground. You have to cut the ground as deep as you can for coals. And that's what they used to heat themselves. Heating. And then it came and they ordered us to get on the trucks. And they took us to the port, Riga port. From there they took us to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with a vessel, an army vessel. They took us to an army vessel. But it's a military vessel.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took us to Stutthof. Stutthof was a gas camp, death camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were working outside of the gas camp. Whoever was strong enough worked [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for everything, you know? Ammunition and, you know, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition, yeah. And we worked for a lot of things, anyhow, for a lot of people there. German groups. German army groups. And then they started up-- there was a sickness going on, typhoid. And I thought this is the end of the line, because people used to die like flies. And all of a sudden a German came in. He said, I need engineers. I need people they know something about engineering and so far. I need couple hundred people. So I was there, naturally. I wanted to get out of this camp. And they ask me questions. I said, yeah, I went to school and all this. I knew already what to say because I did not go to school. I had to lie to them that I went to school. And they took us to Magdeburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you start with Magdeburg, let me back you up a little bit. When you were in Stutthof, describe to me what the daily life was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily life was, first of all, to work, normally. Second, they used to bring in thousands of thousands of women. Naked, only with a blanket on top of them. And they stood in the lines to be gassed. They didn't know they were getting into this. We didn't know either because we-- when we went into the same rooms every other day or whatever, we didn't know that it's going to be gas or water. So it was just pure luck. And those thousands of women and men. Mostly women. But in there, never get out. That was daily life, really. In the morning, [INAUDIBLE] in the morning you have to get up and stand in line, whether it rains or it snows or whatever. You didn't have shoes, doesn't matter. You had holes in your shoes, didn't matter. Food, they gave you a coffee in the morning and they gave us, they called it a kommissbrot. Bread which cuts in four pieces for four people in 24 hours. That was my daily life in Stutthof. I'm not talking about beating up. If any German wants to, he went by and he didn't like your face, he beat you up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were very, very rude and they were murderers. They killed in Stutthof. If there was somebody left alive, a limping one which didn't limp so much, and they caught him in the eye, they have him kneed. And they knocked him, a bullet in his neck. That was the daily life. It was normal life for us. We got used to that kind of life. And that's it. We came in to work and we did what we had to do. And we've been treated like worse than slaves. I don't know. I cannot describe. Slave work-- slaves-- and they worked. They got beaten, but they didn't get killed, as I understand, here. They didn't kill them as much as we got killed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was Stutthof. We couldn't run away from there now because there was German Volksdeutscher. They was Germans, but they were really Poles but they were Germans. And they wouldn't give you the day of light. They wouldn't give you nothing. If you went somehow to beg for a piece of bread, they slammed the door in front of you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Finish with Stutthof. Go on to Magdeburg. Magdeburg, we had-- also a labor camp. No gas, no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no killings. They wanted to get out of us as much as they could. They got trains with coal. Trains with bread. Trains with everything. It went to the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave each one of us, who, you looked healthy or was whatever, they gave us a whole wagon full with coals to unload with a shovel. I could do it, but some people didn't do it, so I went to help them to do it. And how did we eat? We didn't eat. How did we-- how can we exist? So we ate what the pigs ate. The pigs. What they ate, that's what we ate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were instances-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was going to work, a German who was a corporal, he was drinking a lot. He was saying to me, you know, I'll let you go to beg for a piece of bread if you bring me some whiskey. And he let me go and I brought him some whiskey. And at the same time I brought myself a piece of bread or whatever I could beg for or steal. I did everything to survive. That's why I'm here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we worked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our life was miserable. We had to-- the day off was Sunday, and we had to clean the barracks and clean the shit houses and-- of theirs, of the Germans. We had to clean their tables. We had to-- we were slaves. Worse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day they start bombing. The Americans and British and Russians start bombing 24 hours in a row. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We ran to shelters. The German didn't let us in the shelter. But we went into the shelters. They needed us to work, so they didn't want to kill us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: One day [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the war started in the city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the city-- the Americans came in, in the city, and liberated us. But how did I got to be liberated? We were listening to the-- not sirens. When the airplanes are coming. What do you call it? Sirens.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sirens.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sirens. I said 'see-rans.' Sirens. Used to be-- we start to run. Where are we running? We don't know where we're running. And some-- but the Germans themselves didn't want us to be alive when the Americans are coming in. So they put us all together in the yard and they told us that the war is not over yet, but we want to get you deeper in Germany. And so we have to go to the Elbe. Elbe was a big lake. Whatever you call it. It's a very, very big lake. I think it's called-- it's different than lake. And I knew that we're going to be finished in this lake. Somehow rumors went along that they're going to take us to the lake and they're going to kill us in the lake.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as much as I had strength, it was very-- it was already late in '45, yeah. And I jumped the fence, the electrical fence. So did other people. And I got hung on the electrical fence. But somehow my weight or the weight of the current, and I fell down on the bottom.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't walk. My legs were done. So, the other couple people survived. They took me into the sewer. And when they took me into the sewer we waited for the Americans to come in. That was the first time the American that came in. So we went out and they didn't know whether we're Jews or we're Germans. So, they took us by surprise and told us, hand up. You know, hand up. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Right away the Germans had made first aid stations. And I went in the first one in this first aid station because I couldn't walk. And they dig a very big hole in the ground. They put me into the ground until here, until the neck. So the current went out in the ground. That's normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what I-- when I walked out of this ground, of this hole, I walked like I would never walk. And 24 hours later, the Germans pushed out the Americans again to-- out of the city. Because this was a war, yeah? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we hide. We were hiding in the factory where we worked. We were hiding there. And there was a German, an old German, and we took away from him, the clothes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we tied him up. I took his boots. Somebody else took his jacket. Somebody else took his pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went out of the factory like we-- one of us is leading the-- what do you call it? Prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leading the Americans or whoever. One of us is a German and leading the [INAUDIBLE]. You know what [INAUDIBLE] is. And it was another 24 hours or whatever it took and the Americans came in again. So we walked in the streets and they came in with the tanks. And we were, right away, hands up to the world. They took us again as Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And but we got out of it. That was a Jewish coroner and he came to me and he said to me, you say you're Jewish? I said yes. He said, read it. He gave me a Bible to read. That's how I'm alive. [LAUGHS] So, I read the Bible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us away from there. And they said you're free to go any place you want to go. And then I met a lot of people, Jewish people, including my wife. Somebody came to me and said this girl is from a good family, you better watch it. She get out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went into a mayor of the city. And we told this mayor to get out of the house. He said, why should I get out of my house? I said because you took us out of our houses. And we took him out from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had in the-- in the basement he had-- probably not to be too much telling you about, but he had meat, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably 1,000 pounds of meat. They were hanging, pieces of meat. Champagne, cognac. The best. He had everything there. Naturally, we were hungry. So there we are. We're free, we're eating, and we're drinking. And then that time we start organizing trips to Israel. To Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where the rest of your family was besides your father at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of the family was killed in Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- you see, they took probably about 60 or 70,000 people, and they took them 1,000 at a time. They had mass graves there. Mass graves. And they shot them there with automatic weapon. And there is today-- the rumor is there today a stone which says us Latvian Jews are here. This is, you know, like we have it here. The [? Pacific ?] [? theater ?] [INAUDIBLE]. Same things over there. They have it marked. They got killed there. It's definite. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't so definite to me when I went out of the concentration camps and I still had a doubt. I was looking for any place I could look, but they were not there. They were dead. Some of them escaped. When they shot them, they fell. Mass graves. But very little escaped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Coming back to Palestine. We were Russian citizens when we started off. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Americans didn't have no choice that the Russians were asking them that they want their people back. Whatever reason, I don't know what the reason is, but they want them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had a meeting with us, about 1,000 people. And they came in cars and they came in trucks to pick us up. And I was one of them who spoke Russian and I told them that we're going to Palestine. You were always telling us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jud-- no. Jews should go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what they used to say in Russia. From kids to grown ups, they always used to say that. And I said we're not going with you back. We have a choice. We don't have to go with you. We are here on American soil. But whoever wants to go, you know, by himself, that's OK. But we will go to Palestine. And that time we went through Germany and Holland, Dutch, and France. From France we went to Palestine. Not legally, again. Not legally.

speaker: interviewer
text: I met my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somehow I got-- I found out where he is and I met him. And we-- since then-- I was 16 years in Israel, and the rest I'm here, from 1960 until here. So about 34 years in United States. Anything else you want to know? Yes. Tell me how you got to Israel and what it was like when you got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I got in Israel, you know, I was in my youth. I was-- I don't know if people will understand this, but I was a [? betarie. ?] [? Betarie ?] means an organization, a Zionist organization which is a military organization, not just an organization. And we always used to-- our aim was to be Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, when I got to Israel and I got to my father I heard shootings going around. And I asked my father, who is shooting here? The British are occupying the country. They don't let anybody in here unless you're legally. How are they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, I'll tell you what. You just came from concentration camp. I'll introduce you to the guys and you take it from there. Whatever your heart like to do, that's what you do. The next day I was with them. I was organized with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain who 'them' is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Them are, today, the Likud. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they are part of the government, actually, in Knesset. They used to be in the government, the last four years, whatever. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they fought against the British, including me. And we fought against the British until the British get out of it, out of Israel, until the United Nations allowed, finally to have-- for the Jews, a homeland. And by that time my wife was pregnant with my oldest son. My son has born in '48. Exactly when the country was born. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And that was how we got-- But I was also in Jerusalem. I can't remember everything, but this is part of my life which I remember. I remember we went up to Jerusalem. We couldn't get into it because Jordan was-- had occupied Jerusalem. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was in Jerusalem surrounded from the Arabs. And we didn't have no water, so I drank the water from the wells. They have wells in the old city of Jerusalem. They still have wells from thousands of years ago. And nobody drinks the water. But we didn't have a choice, so we drank the water. And I got malaria that time. And somehow they got me out of it and got me to the hospital. I had bad malaria. Bad, very bad malaria, on top of all this what I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I went into the Hadassah-- they took me into the Hadassah-- a doctor-- I just woke up. And they used to wash my body. I woke up and I said, where am I?

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctor said to me, if you're alive, you will live 120 years. This is a malaria from Ethiopia. Bad malaria, bad bug, yeah. It doesn't come anymore. It comes once in a lifetime. But it doesn't come anymore. So I'm fine with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there. We're going to change the tape.
